text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.
I’m inspecting the app structure to find the admin catalog page, current data fetching, and where to wire a form-based Server Action with tagged cache invalidation.

text: The repo is minimal. Next I’m reading the app page and data layer to identify the right cache boundary and implement background revalidation without changing the user flow.

I’ve confirmed this is a small Next app with a stub data source. I’m checking the current Next cache API semantics before editing so the implementation matches the app’s version rather than relying on stale memory.

The relevant API detail is confirmed: for this “keep admins moving, refresh in background” behavior, `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` is the correct invalidation mode, not `updateTag()`. I’m wiring the page around that and enabling cache components if the app hasn’t opted in yet.

I’m editing the data layer to separate cached catalog reads from the sync state, then wiring the page so the form submits instantly while the actual refresh job invalidates the shared `products` tag when it finishes.
The core flow is in place. I’m running a production build now to catch any cache-component or type issues, and I’ll tighten the implementation based on what Next actually accepts.
The build is still running. I’m waiting for the compiler result before making any follow-up changes.

text:
Implemented the admin catalog page with cached product reads and background tag invalidation.

The page now uses an inline Server Action form at [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx:75). Submitting `Sync latest catalog` starts a non-blocking sync job and immediately returns control to the admin. Product reads come from a cached function tagged with `products` in [lib/db.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js:60), so browsing stays fast and views sharing that tag pick up fresh data after the job completes.

The sync flow is in [lib/db.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js:76). It simulates a long-running ERP/PIM refresh, updates the backing catalog data, then calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` so stale catalog entries are served briefly while recomputation happens in the background. I also enabled cache components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts:3) because the tagged `use cache` flow depends on it.

Verified with `npm run build`. Next generated `/` as a static page with `Revalidate 1h` / `Expire 1d`, which confirms the cached catalog path is active. `tsconfig.json` was also updated automatically by Next during build to include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts`.
